mappings.rationale: Directly covers perception including Gestalt principles, perceptual organization, constancy, and the translation of sensory stimulation into organized experience (section C).
Directly covers vision including the retina, transduction process, and perceptual processes of vision (section B.2).
Covers the neural mechanisms underlying attention, sensation, perception, and the processing of sensory information from the environment.
Covers the neural basis of attention, sensory processing, perceptual organization, and responses of the cerebral cortex to stimuli.
Directly relevant to awareness of internal and external events (section A) and the problem of defining attention and conscious experience.
Provides the broad psychological framework for understanding sensation, perception, attention, and environmental influences on behaviour.
Directly covers the sensory experience of pain including cause, function, external signs, qualities, theories, and modes of treatment (section B.4.d).
Directly covers the olfactory sense including receptor form, location, nerve supply, olfactory qualities, and effects on behaviour (section B.8).
A perceptual phenomenon directly relevant to interrelations among the senses (section E.3) and individual differences in perceptual functioning (section C.4).
Covers the physics of sound relevant to audition, including mechanisms of transmission and the physical basis of pitch, loudness, and duration (section B.3).
Covers the physics of light relevant to visual perception, the retinal image, and the transduction process (section B.2).
Relevant to context effects in perception, how previously experienced stimuli influence the observer (section C.3.b).
Relevant to information processing theories of attention (section A.6) and the cognitive processes that interact with perception.
Relevant to hallucinatory experiences (section G.2) and altered perceptual states involving visual and sensory phenomena.
Emotions influence attention and perception; relevant to determinants of attention including temperament and unconscious influences (section A.4).
Covers wave physics underlying both audition (sound waves) and vision (light waves), the physical basis of sensory stimulation.
Relevant to differences in perceptual functioning among individuals (section C.4) and atypical sensory processing.
Relevant to hallucinations (section G.2) including neurological factors and types of hallucinatory experience.
Covers auditory perception and processing of complex sounds, relevant to audition mechanisms and perceptual organization of sound (sections B.3, C).
